Fergie hasn’t been stripped of her dignity, she wilfully lost it long ago.

Sarah Ferguson has been holed-up in a £2,000-a-night pine‑clad chalet in a quiet resort close to the exclusive Mayrlife health clinic in Austria where the super-rich are drawn to its remoteness and privacy. When she was spotted this week, it was the first time she had been seen in months, and the strain of her long absence was etched into an otherwise expressionless face. Asked about the photographs yesterday, a close friend who has remained in text contact with her since her departure from Britain said: 'She has been in the same place all the time, lying low and trying to work out her next move.'

The country's top civil servant, Dame Antonia Romeo, and the Cabinet Office's Cat Little found out last month that Peter Mandelson was appointed US ambassador against security official advice. Embattled Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer insisted he was unaware until Tuesday that the New Labour grandee had been appointed to Washington last year despite failing his vetting. Heidi Cornish, who is standing for Colchester City Council, was a character witness for Luke D'Wit, 36, as he faced charges of murdering Carol and Stephen Baxter in Essex in 2023. Every morning for the best part of 20 years Paul Quinn woke up knowing that another man - Andrew Malkinson - was rotting in jail for a crime that he committed. Yesterday, following a six-week trial at Manchester Crown Court, Quinn was convicted of two counts of rape. He was also found guilty of grievous bodily harm and attempting to choke or strangle his victim to render her unconscious while he attacked her. Quinn's belated conviction will put further pressure on the authorities - and Greater Manchester Police (GMP) in particular - to explain one of the worst miscarriages of justice in British legal history.

When I was going through the menopause, I had a fan on my desk at work and one next to my bed, which I left on all through the night regardless of weather; not menopausal now of course at 73 but that fan is still next to my bed and it's on near enough all night. Whenever I was feeling rather sticky due to sweating, I threw talcum powder on the affected area. It's not a cure but it soaks up the sweat and helps one to feel a little cooler. I used it when sweating long before the menopause came along as well, including on my youngest who often was soaked in sweat (when sleeping) in bed at night. I was never offered HRT so had to make the best of it.

Three years ago, Helen started sweating so profusely overnight that she would wake up, her hair drenched, and have to flip over her pillow. It was a distressing symptom of the menopause.
  • 2 comments
  • share How to end the misery of hot flushes and sweating without HRT

My wife told me over breakfast she dreamed I'd given her a diamond ring "I wonder what it means," she said. I smiled but said nothing and left for work. That evening I brought her a small gift-wrapped package. Oh you should have seen how excited she was! Brought a tear to my eye. She opened the present with trembling hands: a little book titled "How to interpret your dreams."

It's something many of us regularly think about - what happens when you're about to die? Now, scientists have revealed the dreams you'll probably have as you near your end.
  • 111 comments
  • 1 video

Stop with the indoctrination, it isn't any good for the environment when the world's worst polluter are building them all. China 1270 coal fired power stations, UK 0 coal fired power stations but let's ship cars halfway across the world and pretend it's eco

its all to do with the cameras focal length its not that steep at all

One of the steepest bridges in the world is so daunting that drivers say it gives them nightmares. Dubbed the 'rollercoaster bridge', the Eshima Ohashi in Japan links Matsue in Shimane Prefecture with Sakaiminato in Tottori Prefecture. The £119million crossing is used by thousands of motorists each day and rises sharply to allow ships to pass underneath.
  • 171 comments
  • share Japan's 'rollercoaster' bridge looks so steep drivers have nightmares

Wet and windy in Scotland! Who would have thought?? May I suggest to these ladies that a raincoat is probably the most important article of clothing to possess for such occasions??

Umbrellas proved to be one of the most important accessory of the day, with the heavens opening over the Ayr Race Course in Ayrshire, Scotland.

Email

The Apprentice curse: Why winning the show isn't all it's cracked up to be with stars facing financial woes and parting ways with Lord Sugar - as influencer Karishma Vijay nabs his £250k investment

This year marks the 20th series of The Apprentice, as once again 20 candidates enter Lord Alan Sugar's boardroom for the 'toughest 12 weeks of their life'.  Beginning back in 2005, the original prize for the hit BBC show was a £100,000 a year 12 month job working for Lord Sugar, yet later in 2011 this was changed to a £250,000 business partnership.  And over the years the show has seen everything, from sweet and brownie brands to recruitment, plumbing and even a cosmetic clinic chain.
  • 72 comments
  • 23 shares The Apprentice curse: Why winning isn't all it's cracked up to be
